_comment_: Agent sandbox seccomp profile (05-SEC §13).
Strategy: default=ALLOW, explicit deny for a curated list of dangerous syscalls.
Why not default=ERRNO + full whitelist?
- Docker's own default.json whitelists ~300+ syscalls to let a generic Linux
userspace process run; keeping that list in sync with kernel/libc updates
has non-trivial maintenance cost.
- The container already runs as uid=1000, with CapDrop=ALL and
no-new-privileges; several of the ALLOW-by-default syscalls below require
capabilities we don't grant, so they would EPERM at kernel level anyway.
- Our supervisor validates profiles at create time: a defaultAction=ALLOW
profile with zero deny rules is refused (see normalizeSeccompOption).
What the deny list covers (high-impact categories):
- Kernel module load/unload: init_module / finit_module / delete_module /
create_module / query_module
- Kernel live-patch / reboot: reboot, kexec_load, kexec_file_load
- Time manipulation: adjtimex, clock_adjtime, clock_settime, settimeofday,
stime  (prevents container from subtly shifting host clock)
- Mount / pivot / chroot: mount, umount, umount2, pivot_root, chroot,
move_mount, mount_setattr, open_tree, fsopen, fsmount, fsconfig, fspick
(chroot also needs CAP_SYS_CHROOT — doubly blocked)
- Namespaces: unshare, setns, clone3, clone(CLONE_NEW*)  (creating
new namespaces is an escape vector; clone3 takes flags via struct
pointer which seccomp can't dereference, so it's denied wholesale)
- Tracing / eBPF: ptrace, bpf, perf_event_open, process_vm_readv,
process_vm_writev, kcmp  (block cross-process inspection)
- Swap / quota: swapon, swapoff, quotactl, quotactl_fd
- System accounting: acct
- Execution domain: personality  (flipping personality allows exploiting
different ABI handling)
- I/O port: iopl, ioperm  (direct hardware access, needs CAP_SYS_RAWIO anyway)
- Legacy kernel APIs: nfsservctl, lookup_dcookie, vmsplice, bdflush,
ustat, sysfs, _sysctl
- IPC namespace / key mgmt: add_key, request_key, keyctl  (blocks
keyring-based credential stashing)
- Deprecated shared library loader: uselib
What we deliberately do NOT deny despite being listed in some hardening guides:
- capset: runc/containerd uses it during container init to drop the
capability bounding set (CapDrop=ALL is implemented via capset). Denying
it makes the container fail with "can't apply capabilities".
- clone (without CLONE_NEW* bits): pthread_create, popen, subprocess
spawning — universal userspace needs.
What we deliberately keep allowed:
- fork/clone/clone3/execve/execveat: required by bun, git subprocess
- mmap/mprotect/mremap/madvise: required by Node/Bun runtime
- socket/connect/bind: agent needs to reach transparent proxy
- ioctl (general): a ton of legitimate uses; rely on cap drop + readonly fs
This profile passes openclaude/agent-sandbox supervisor validation:
- defaultAction is SCMP_ACT_ALLOW (explicit)
- syscalls list is non-empty AND contains SCMP_ACT_ERRNO entries
Upgrade path: once the agent's syscall needs have been profiled over a few
weeks of production traffic, we can flip to default=ERRNO + whitelist and
drop this explicit deny list.

syscalls._note: Kernel module lifecycle — agent has zero legitimate need to touch modules
System lifecycle / kexec (crash-kernel load) — absolutely not from a container
Time manipulation — container must not shift host clock
Mount / fsopen family — do not allow remount or new mount points
Namespace manipulation — blocks setup of a secondary sandbox inside ours

syscalls._note_clone3: clone3 takes flags inside a struct clone_args *, which seccomp cannot dereference. Deny it outright with ENOSYS(38) — NOT EPERM(1) — so glibc >= 2.34 detects 'kernel lacks clone3' and falls back to the `clone` path (pthread_create / popen / git-child-process). Returning EPERM here breaks pthread_create on debian bookworm / node:22-slim because glibc only triggers the fallback on ENOSYS.

syscalls._note_clone_newuser: clone(CLONE_NEWUSER=0x10000000) — user namespace creation is the primary escape vector paired with setuid binaries. One rule per CLONE_NEW* bit because SCMP_CMP_MASKED_EQ matches (arg & value) == valueTwo exactly; deny-any-bit-set is expressed as N rules OR'd via the libseccomp ruleset semantics.

syscalls._note: Swap / quota / accounting — host-level resource ops
Direct hardware I/O — needs CAP_SYS_RAWIO which we drop anyway
Legacy / deprecated kernel APIs with known bad surface
Kernel keyring — prevent credential stashing outside process mem

clone3 denied above — this is a second explicit deny for `kcmp` alias if any. No-op rule kept as a placeholder so the syscalls list always has at least one entry after editing; supervisor's normalizeSeccompOption requires the list to be non-empty AND contain a deny rule. Safe to remove once other deny entries are guaranteed stable.
